informal: Suppose $E\subset\mathbb{R}^k$ is uncountable, and let $P$ be the set of condensation points of $E$. Prove that $P$ is perfect.
lean:
theorem Rudin_exercise_2_27a (k : ℕ) (E P : Set (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin k)))
  (hE : ¬ Set.Countable E)
  (hP : P = {x | ∀ U ∈ 𝓝 x, ¬ Set.Countable (U ∩ E)}) :
  Perfect P
reasoning: The ambient space EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin k) is ℝ^k. E is an arbitrary subset, with the hypothesis ¬Set.Countable E corresponding to "uncountable". P is defined as {x | ∀ U ∈ 𝓝 x, ¬Countable (U ∩ E)}, which is the standard definition of condensation points (every neighborhood of x meets E in an uncountable set). The conclusion `Perfect P` in Mathlib means P is closed and every point is an accumulation point, matching "P is perfect". The quantifier structure and types align with the informal statement.
